raw: {"weights": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.3, "R1.6": 0.3, "R1.7": 0.3, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 1.0, "RX.5": 1.0}, "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"], "rationale": {"R1.1": "The artifact contains an explicit contribution claim (per-query routing, first in climate emulation) and a gap statement (static partitioning vs. adaptive); reconstructing whether the thesis is well-formed is a plausible primary intent of 'What do you think?'", "R1.2": "A load-bearing logical argument is made about non-stationarity robustness via internal consistency of the difficulty score with emulator inputs — this chain of reasoning is substantive and disputable, making logical scrutiny a plausible primary intent.", "R1.3": "Specific quantitative claims are asserted (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, threshold calibrated on 2014–2017 valid through SSP5-8.5) with only brief methodological grounding; checking whether these claims are adequately backed is a plausible primary intent.", "R1.4": "'To our knowledge, this is the first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation' is an explicit novelty claim; assessing its accuracy against related work (mixture-of-experts, cascade models, neural-statistical hybrids) is a plausible primary intent."}, "clarification_needed": true, "clarification_question": "Is your primary interest (a) whether the scientific and logical arguments hold up (e.g., the non-stationarity robustness claim), (b) whether the quantitative results are adequately supported by the described experiments, or (c) whether the novelty claim ('first work') is defensible against related literature?"}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact contains an explicit contribution claim (per-query routing, first in climate emulation) and a gap statement (static partitioning vs. adaptive); reconstructing whether the thesis is well-formed is a plausible primary intent of 'What do you think?'
rationale.R1.2: A load-bearing logical argument is made about non-stationarity robustness via internal consistency of the difficulty score with emulator inputs — this chain of reasoning is substantive and disputable, making logical scrutiny a plausible primary intent.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims are asserted (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, threshold calibrated on 2014–2017 valid through SSP5-8.5) with only brief methodological grounding; checking whether these claims are adequately backed is a plausible primary intent.
rationale.R1.4: 'To our knowledge, this is the first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation' is an explicit novelty claim; assessing its accuracy against related work (mixture-of-experts, cascade models, neural-statistical hybrids) is a plausible primary intent.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary interest (a) whether the scientific and logical arguments hold up (e.g., the non-stationarity robustness claim), (b) whether the quantitative results are adequately supported by the described experiments, or (c) whether the novelty claim ('first work') is defensible against related literature?
